entries.title: Centrelink said I owe money or changed my payment

reviewable.basis: Most Centrelink decisions can be reviewed.

pathways.howCounted: from the date of the decision
pathways.name: Internal review (ask a review officer to look again)
pathways.deadline: There is a time limit — VERIFY current timeframe + source before launch
pathways.howToStart: ask Services Australia for a formal review of the decision

pathways.name: External merits review (an independent tribunal looks at it)
pathways.deadline: There is a time limit — VERIFY current timeframe + source before launch

rightToReasons.how: you can ask Services Australia for the reasons for the decision in writing

groundsOrCriteria: the decision used wrong information, such as income or dates (VERIFY framing + source)
the law or the payment rate was applied incorrectly (VERIFY)
your actual circumstances were not taken into account (VERIFY)
evidenceChecklist: payslips or income records for the period in question
letters and dates from Centrelink about the decision
anything that shows your actual circumstances at the time

getHelp.who: your state or territory Legal Aid commission

entries.plainLanguageExplainer: Centrelink has made a decision about your payment, or says you owe money. In most cases you can ask them to look at it again, and if you still disagree you can ask an independent tribunal to review it for free. There is a time limit, so it helps to find out the date early.
entries.body:
Centrelink (Services Australia) has made a decision about your payment, or says you
have a debt. In most cases you can ask them to look at the decision again, and if you
still disagree you can ask an independent tribunal to review it — and that review is
free. There is a time limit, so it helps to find out the date early.

> All of the figures and time limits in this entry are marked **VERIFY** and must be
> confirmed against the cited source by a human before launch. Until then the app does
> not state a specific deadline — it explains that a time limit applies and routes the
> person to a free service that can confirm it.

entries.title: A Commonwealth (federal) government decision I disagree with

reviewable.basis: Many — but not all — Commonwealth decisions can be reviewed. Whether yours can, and how, depends on the law that the decision was made under.

pathways.howCounted: usually from the decision date, if a time limit applies
pathways.name: Internal review (ask the agency to look again)
pathways.body: the agency that made the decision
pathways.deadline: There may be a time limit — VERIFY whether one applies + source
pathways.howToStart: ask the agency in writing to review the decision
pathways.source: VERIFY (the Act the decision was made under)

pathways.howCounted: usually from the decision being reviewed
pathways.name: External merits review (an independent tribunal looks at it)
pathways.deadline: There is usually a time limit — VERIFY current timeframe + source
pathways.howToStart: check whether the decision is one the ART can review, then apply

pathways.name: Complaint
pathways.howToStart: make a complaint online if you think the agency acted unfairly

rightToReasons.how: for many Commonwealth decisions you can ask, in writing, for a statement of reasons

groundsOrCriteria: whether the decision-maker applied the right law and criteria (VERIFY)
whether all the relevant facts about your situation were considered (VERIFY)
evidenceChecklist: the decision letter and any reference numbers
the date the decision was made or sent
anything that shows your actual circumstances

getHelp.who: your state or territory Legal Aid commission

entries.plainLanguageExplainer: This is a general guide for a decision made by a federal government office. Many government decisions can be reviewed if you disagree, but not all of them. The time limits and steps depend on the law behind the decision. Because the details matter, it helps to check with a free legal service. They can tell you if your decision can be reviewed, and by when.
sources: VERIFY: art.gov.au — what decisions can be reviewed
entries.body:
This is a general guide for a decision made by a Commonwealth (federal) government
agency when there isn't a more specific guide for your exact situation. Many
government decisions can be reviewed, but not all of them, and the time limits and
steps depend on the law the decision was made under. Because the details matter, the
safest next step is to talk to a free legal service, who can confirm whether your
decision can be reviewed and by when.

> This is the fallback entry. It deliberately stays general and routes the person to
> human help rather than asserting a pathway or deadline that may not apply to them.

entries.title: The NDIS made a decision about my plan or my eligibility

reviewable.basis: NDIS decisions have an internal review, then an external review path.

pathways.name: Internal review (review of a reviewable decision)
pathways.deadline: There is a time limit — VERIFY current timeframe + source before launch
pathways.howToStart: ask the NDIA to review the decision
pathways.source: VERIFY (ndis.gov.au / National Disability Insurance Scheme Act)

pathways.name: External merits review (an independent tribunal looks at it)
pathways.deadline: There is a time limit — VERIFY current timeframe + source before launch
pathways.howToStart: apply to the ART; free advocacy may be available to help you

rightToReasons.how: you can ask the NDIA for the reasons for the decision

groundsOrCriteria: whether a support meets the 'reasonable and necessary' test (VERIFY exact criteria + source)
new evidence about your needs that wasn't considered (VERIFY)
evidenceChecklist: reports from your treating professionals (doctors, therapists)
evidence of how a support helps you take part in daily life, work or study
quotes or assessments for the supports you need

getHelp.who: free, funded advocates who help with NDIS reviews

entries.plainLanguageExplainer: The NDIA has made a decision about whether you can access the NDIS, or about what is in your plan. You can ask the NDIA to review the decision, and if you still disagree you can ask an independent tribunal to review it for free. Free advocates can help you. There is a time limit, so it helps to find out the date early.
sources: VERIFY: National Disability Insurance Scheme Act 2013 (Cth)
entries.body:
The NDIA has made a decision about whether you can access the NDIS, or about what is
in your plan. You can ask the NDIA to review the decision, and if you still disagree
you can ask an independent tribunal to review it for free. Free advocates can help you
through the process. There is a time limit, so it helps to find out the date early.

> All figures and time limits here are marked **VERIFY** and must be confirmed by a
> human before launch. The app does not state a specific deadline until that happens.
